title: Fallback Paths
breadcrumbs: Assignments for Remote Services and Reports
text: Fallback Paths Uniface enables you to define alternative paths for executing remote components. In the client assignment file, you can use path-to-connector assignments to define a sequence of fallback paths to be used if the first path is not available. Interactive failures, such as incorrectly entering a user name or password, do not activate the fallback mechanism. If the initial path fails to open, an error is reported in the message frame and Uniface tries to open the first alternative path specified. If that path fails to open, an error is again reported and Uniface tries to open the second alternative path. This can be continued without a practical limit. Rules for Specifying Fallback Paths To specify fallback assignments, follow these rules: Use only path-to-connector assignments. The target cannot be another path. Number each fallback assignment sequentially, beginning with 1. For example: [PATHS ] $Path = TCP: Host | UserName | Password $Path [1] = TCP: HostOne | UserName | Password $Path [2] = TCP: HostTwo | UserName | Password Do not allow gaps in the numbering sequence. Errors are reported if these rules are not followed. Example: Fallback Paths In the following example, two alternative paths are provided for the path $SRU. If the logon to node phoenix does not succeed, Uniface tries to open the fallback path to roc . A Log On form is presented for user kaye on node roc . If the user cannot log on to roc and clicks Cancel, Uniface attempts to open the second fallback path. If that logon attempt also fails, an error is reported to the process trying to open the path. [PATHS] $SRU = TCP:phoenix|kaye|birdbrain $SRU[1] = TCP:roc|kaye|? $SRU[2] = TCP:emu|general|? Copy Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
